  (IV) (as HCl) 
 3.2 mg/mL, 250 mL D5W (pre-mixed)  
  (IV) (as HCl) 
 
A direct -acting sympathomimetic which acts on 
beta1 receptors in cardiac muscles, leading to 
increased contractility with little effect on rate, 
and may be given for short periods in the 
treatment of severe heart failure; it also has 
an advantage over others by causing renal 
vasodilation, thus preserving r enal perfusion 
and renal function. 
 
Indications: Hypovolemic shock and hemorrhagic 
shock as adjuvant therapy to volume r e-
placement; cardiogenic shock ( where systolic 
BP <90 in adults).  
 
Contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to 
dopamine or any of the excipients; ischemic 
heart disease; tachyarrhythmias, ventricular 
fibrillation; pheochromocytoma; hyperthyroi d-
ism. 
 
Dose: 
Hypovolemic and hemorrhagic shock as adjuvant 
therapy to volume replacement, by IV infusion 
into a large vein, ADULT, initially 5 m i-
crograms/kg per minute, gradually increased 
by 5 to 10 micrograms/kg per minute accor d-
ing to blood pressure, cardiac output, and 
urine output (seriously -ill patients, up to 20 
micrograms/kg per minute); CHILD, 2 to 10 
micrograms/kg per minute depending on pa-
tient response. 
DILUTION AND ADMINISTRATION. See Administration. 
 
Dose Adjustments: 
Renal and Hepatic Impairment: 
For mild -to-moderate impairment, drug should be 
used with caution, and dose reduction may be 
warranted; for severe impairment, the patient 
should be referred to a specialist. 
 
Precautions: 
 
 
WARNING: This may cause peripheral ischemia in patients 
with history of occlusive vascular disease (e.g., ather-
osclerosis and Raynaud’s syndrome). In case of ex-
travasation causing peripheral ischemia, use phen-
tolamine for local infiltration. 
 
NOTE: Dosage is critical  – at low doses, it stimulates 
myocardial contractility and increases cardiac output; 
however, higher doses (more than 5 m i-
crograms/kg/minute) cause vasoconstriction, w hich 
increases blood pressure and may a lso cause wors-
ening of heart failure. 
Close monitoring of arterial and venous pressure 
and continuous ECG should be performed; 
treatment with sympathomimetics should be 
guided by hemodynamic monitoring (individu-
alize treatment depending on clinical r e-
sponse); use low dose in cardiogenic shock 
due to MI; prolonged use of sympathomimet-
ics may result in diminution of therapeutic ef-
fect (downregulation of receptors) ; dispropo r-
tionate increase in diastolic pressure.  
Correct hypovolemia before, and maintain blood 
volume during the treatment; correct hypoxia, 
hypercapnia, and metabolic acidosis before or 
at the same time that treatment is started  
(may possibly reduce the effectiveness and/or 
increase the incidence of adverse effects of 
dopamine); pulmonary hypertension (may be 
worsened due to dopamine -induced pulm o-
nary vasoconstriction) ; suppresses pituitary 
secretion of thyroid -stimulating hormone, 
growth hormone and prolactin.  
Elderly; pregnancy (limited human data available). 
 
Adverse Drug Reactions: 
Common: Anginal pain, chest pain, dyspnea, ec-
topic beats, flushing, headache, hypotension 
with dizziness, nausea, palpitations, periphe r-
al vasoconstriction, vomiting, tachycardia.  
Less Common:  Abnormal ventricular conduction, 
bradycardia, extravasation (this may cause 
necrosis and sloughing of surrounding tissue) , 
fainting, gangrene, hypertension, mydriasis, 
piloerection, uremia.  
Rare: Allergic reactions, ventricular arrhythmia. 
 
Drug Interactions: 
Monitor closely with: 
Alpha-adrenergic blockin g agents – peripheral 
vasoconstriction caused by high doses of d o-
pamine is antagonized. 
MAO Inhibitors (e.g., linezolid, phenelzine, and 
tranylcypromine) – these inhibit metabolism of 
dopamine; they may prolong and intensify its 
effects. 
Phenytoin – co- administration in patients with d o-
pamine has resulted in hypotension and brad-
ycardia. 
Avoid concomitant use with: 
Beta-adrenergic blocking agents (e.g., propranolol 
and metoprolol) – the cardiac effects of do-
pamine are antagonized. 
Chlorpromazine – antagonism of hypertensive 
effect. 
Fluphenazine – antagonism of hypertensive effect. 
Haloperidol – antagonism of hypertensive effect. 
Metoclopramide – this decreases level of dopamine 
by inhibiting GI absorption. 
Vasoconstrictors (e.g., ergot alkaloids) – these may 
increase peripheral vasoconstriction and the 
risk of peripheral ischemia. 
 
Administration: The solution should be diluted 
before administration (usual dilution is 1.6 or 
3.2 mg/mL) in dextrose 5% or sodium chloride 
0.9%. A lkaline solutions, such as sodium bi-
carbonate (NaHCO 3), should not be added 
(since the drug will be inactivated). Utilize a

67 
 
large vein high up in the limb, preferably the 
arm, to avoid tissue necrosis. 
 
Pregnancy Category: C 
 
 
 
 EPINEPHRINE 
 
Inj.: 1:1000 (1 mg/mL) (SC, IM, IV) or 1:10,000  (1 
mg/10 mL) (IV) 
 
A direct-acting, mixed alpha- and beta-adrenoceptor 
agonist; a sympathomimetic catecholamine, 
which is a potent cardiac stimulant vasocon-
strictor and bronchodilator. 
NOTE: Vasodilator at low dose (beta 2  receptors); but, 
vasoconstrictor at high dose (alpha receptors). 
 
Indications: Severe anaphylactic reactions; severe 
angioedema with laryngeal swelling or airway 
obstruction; cardiac arrest. 
 
Contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to 
epinephrine or any of the excipients;  phe o-
chromocytoma; use in local anesthesia of fi n-
gers, toes, ears, nose or genitalia; shock; or-
ganic heart disease or cardiac dilatation; 
closed-angle glaucoma; labor. 
NOTE: However, there are no absolute contraindications to 
the use of epinephrine in life-threatening allergic reac-
tions. 
 
Dose:  
Anaphylaxis, by IM or SC injection of 1:1,000 epi-
nephrine injection, ADULT and ADOLES-
CENT, 500 micrograms (0.5 mL); INFANT <6 
months, 50 micrograms (0.05 mL); CHILD 6 
months-6 years, 120 micrograms (0.12 mL), 
6-12 years, 250 micrograms (0.25 mL) or for 
CHILDREN, 10 micrograms (0.01 mL of 
1:1,000 solution)/kg bodyweight SC, repeated 
if necessary at intervals of 20 minutes to four 
(4) hours depending on the response of the 
patient and the severity of the condition.  Sin-
gle pediatric doses should not exceed 500 
micrograms. 
Anaphylaxis, by slow IV injection of 1:10,000 (given 
at a rate of 1 mL/minute) epinephrine injec-
tion; this route should be reserved for severely 
ill patients when there is doubt about the ade-
quacy of circulation and absorption from the 
IM site, ADULT, 500 micrograms (5 mL); 
CHILD, 10 micrograms/kg (0.1 mL/kg), given 
over several minutes. 
Cardiac arrest (asystole), ADULT, the recommend-
ed dose is 1 mg IV, using 10 mL of the 
1:10,000 solution. This may be repeated every 
3-5 minutes. If given through a peripheral line, 
each dose should be followed by a flush of 20 
mL of IV fluid to ensure delivery of the drug to 
the central compartment. Intracardiac admi n-
istration is  no longer recommended; CHIL-
DREN, the recommended dose is 10 m i-
crograms (0.1 mL of the 1:10,000 solution)/kg 
body weight administered IV. This may be r e-
peated every 3-5 minutes. 
 
Dose Adjustment: No information found. 
 
Precautions:  
Cerebrovascular disease (increased risk of periph-
eral ischemia or stroke) ; hypovolemia (correct 
before using epinephrine); acidosis, hype r-
capnia, or hypoxia (may reduce the effectiv e-
ness, and increase the incidence of adverse 
effects of epinephrine) ; heart diseases (e.g., 
ischemic heart disease, heart failure, other a r-
rhythmias; increased risk of arrhythmias, an-
gina and myocardial ischemia); aortic stenosis 
and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (possibly in-
creased outflow obstruction) ; arrhythmias (r e-
duce dose; monitor closely); pulmonary hype r-
tension (may worsen du e to pulmonary vas o-
constriction);  
Elderly; hyperthyroidism, diabetes mellitus (adverse 
reactions are more likely to occur) ; second 
stage of labor. 
 
Adverse Drug Reactions: 
Common: Anxiety, dizziness, dyspnea, fear, hea d-
ache, hyperglycemia, nausea, pallor,  palpita-
tions, restlessness, sweating, tachycardia, 
tremor, vomiting, weakness.  
Less Common:  Angina, cerebral hemorrhage, 
excessive increase in blood pressure, hyper-
tension, peripheral ischemia and necrosis (at 
infusion site), pulmonary edema, ventricular  
arrhythmias. 
Rare: Allergic reactions. 
 
Drug Interactions:  
NOTE: Alpha-adrenergic blockers antagonize vasoconstric t-
ing and hypertensive effects of epinephrine. 
Monitor closely with: 
Drugs causing potassium loss (e.g., corticosteroids, 
potassium-depleting diuretics, aminophylline, 
theophylline) – these may po tentiate the 
hypokalemic effect of epinephrine.  
Ergot Alkaloids – these may reverse the pressor 
effects of epinephrine. 
Oxytocin – there is risk of hypertension due to en-
hanced vasopressor effect of epinephrine. 
Tricyclic Antidepressants – these block the upt ake 
of catecholamines; can intensify and prolong 
epinephrine’s effects (since uptake is one 
mechanism by which catecholamines are ter-
minated); patients having TCAs are more sus-
ceptible to arrhythmias (reduced dose of epi-
nephrine may be necessary). 
Avoid concomitant use with:  
Alpha-blockers – these can prevent receptor activ a-
tion by epinephrine (antagonize vasoconstrict-
ing and hypertensive effects of epinephrine). 
Beta-blockers – these can prevent receptor activa-
tion by epinephrine; vasoconstrictor effect of 
epinephrine predominates; hypertension fol-
lowed by bradycardia may occur. 
Hypoglycemic agents – may lead to loss of blood 
sugar control in diabetic patients when taken 
concomitantly with these drugs (epinephrine 
induces hyperglycemia). 
 
Administration: Epinephrine is best administered 
as an SC or IM injection into the anterolateral 
aspect of the middle third of the thigh for ana-
phylaxis, IV for cardiac arrest or emergency 
situations

68 
 
NOTE: If central line is used, infusion pump required. Do not 
mix with alkaline solutions. Discard after 24 hours or if 
solution is discolored or contains precipitate.  
 
Pregnancy Category: C 
 
 
ANTI-ANGINALS 
 
 
 ISOSORBIDE DINITRATE (ISDN) 
 
Oral: 5 mg, 10 mg and 20 mg tablet 
 20 mg and 40 mg MR tablet/capsule 
Sublingual: 5 mg tablet 
Inj.: 1 mg/mL, 10 mL ampul (IV) 
 
An organic nitrate vasodilator whose stability in 
storage is better than glyceryl trinitrate, and is 
useful in patients who require nitrates infr e-
quently; it has a slower onset of action, but 
longer duration. 
 
Indications: Prophylaxis and treatment of angina; 
left ventricular failure. 
 
Contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to 
nitrates or any of the excipients; hypotension; 
hypovolemia; hypertrophic obstructive cardi-
omyopathy, aortic stenosis, cardiac tam-
ponade, constrictive pericarditis, mitral steno-
sis; marked anemia; head trauma; cerebral 
hemorrhage; angle-closure glaucoma. 
 For extended release formulation: GI hype r-
motility, malabsorption syndrome.  
 
Dose:  
Angina (acute attack), sublingually, ADULT, 2.5 to 5 
mg, repeated every 5-10 minutes as required  
for 3 doses. NOTE: Inability to relieve chest 
pain after 3 doses may signal acute myocar-
dial infarction which will need immediate hos-
pitalization. IV infusion may be started, while 
awaiting transfer to the hospital , at 1 mg/hour 
and titrated upward to 4 mg/hour.  
Angina prophylaxis, by mouth, ADULT, for regular 
release tablet, start at 5- 20 mg twice to thrice 
daily, usual maintenance do se is 10 -40 mg 
twice to thrice daily, (maximum,  240 mg) ; 
for extended release tablet, 40-80 mg once or 
twice daily, space twice daily dose 6 hours 
apart, (maximum, 160 mg/day ). NOTE: Pr o-
vide nitrate -free interval daily (14 hours for 
regular release and 18 hours for extended r e-
lease) to avoid development of tolerance.  
Prevention of acute angina before a precipitating 
activity, sublingually, ADULT, 5 -10 mg taken 
10 minutes before activity. 
Prevention of chronic angina, by mouth, ADULT, 
10-40 mg up to 3 times daily. 
Treatment of acute heart failure,  sublingually, 
ADULT, 5-10 mg every 2 hours or as needed. 
Treatment of chronic heart failure, by mouth, 
ADULT, 20-40 mg 4 times daily (with hydral a-
zine). 
 
 
 
 
 
Dose Adjustments:  
Renal Impairment:  
Single oral doses of the drug, in patients with renal 
impairment, are not different from those with 
normal renal function; but, consider reducing 
dose for chronic use (since significant acc u-
mulation of the active metabolites may occur). 
Hepatic Impairment:  
Consider reducing dose since ISDN is, in part, 
metabolized by the liver. 
 
Precautions:  
Severe hepatic impairment; renal impairment; hyp o-
thyroidism; recent history of MI; malnutrition; 
hypothermia. 
Pregnancy (may cross placenta – avoid medication 
unless potential benefit outweighs risk). 
TOLERANCE. Patients taking ISDN for long-term manage-
ment of angina may often develop tolerance to the an-
ti-anginal effect; this can be avoided by giving the 
second of 2 daily doses of longer -acting oral presen-
tations after an 8- hour rather than a 12 -hour interval, 
thus ensuring a nitrate-free interval each day. 
 
Adverse Drug Reactions: 
Common: Dizziness, fainting, flushing, hypoten-
sion, including orthostatic hypotension ; palpi-
tations, peripheral edema, tachycardia, thro b-
bing headache. 
Less Common:  Heartburn, hypoxemia, nausea, 
rash, rebound angina, syncope, vomiting. 
Rare: Angle-closure glaucoma. 
 
Drug Interactions:  
Monitor closely with: 
Atropine – possibly reduced effect of sublingual 
ISDN tablets (failure to dissolve under the 
tongue owing to dry mouth). 
Drugs which reduce blood pressure (see Appendix 
– Table D) – nitrates cause hypotension and, 
if given with these drugs, may have enhanced 
hypotensive effects. 
Avoid concomitant use with: 
Phosphodiesterase Inhibitors (e.g., sildenafil) – 
nitrates potentiate the hypotensive effects of 
phosphodiesterase inhibitors; combinations 
may result in profound hypotension or my o-
cardial infarction.  
Contraceptives, Oral – antagonism of hypotensive 
effect. 
Dexamethasone – antagonism of hypotensive ef-
fect. 
Hydrocortisone – antagonism of hypotensive effect. 
Ibuprofen – antagonism of hypotensive effect. 
Prednisolone – antagonism of hypotensive effect. 
 
Administration: Should be taken on an empty 
stomach ½ hour before meals. For sublingual  
tablets, sit or lie down before use (as the tab-
let may cause dizziness ); place the appropr i-
ate dose under the tongue; do not swallow; af-
ter the pain has been relieved, the patient may 
spit out or swallow what is left of the tablet to 
avoid adverse effects, such as headache. For 
IV infusion, mix 10 mL (equivalent to 10 mg) in 
90 mL NSS.  
 
Pregnancy Category: C

DILTIAZEM 
 
Oral: 30 mg and 60 mg tablet (as hydrochloride) 
 60 mg, 90 mg, 120 mg and 180 mg MR cap-
sule (as hydrochloride) 
 90 mg, 120 mg and 180 mg MR tablet (as 
hydrochloride) 
 
A nondihydropyridine, calcium -channel blocker, 
which has a negative chronotropic effect, thus 
decreasing the workload of the heart, and 
consequently reducing its oxygen requir e-
ments. 
 
Indications: Prophylaxis and treatment of angina; 
hypertension. 
 
Contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to 
diltiazem or any component of the formulation; 
marked bradycardia (below 40 beats/minute); 
sick sinus syndrome (except in the presence 
of a pacemaker); severe hypotension ( <90 
mmHg systolic); congestive heart failure; 
acute porphyria; second- or third-degree atrio-
ventricular block (without pacemaker); Wolff -
Parkinson-White syndrome; acute MI and 
pulmonary congestion; pregnancy. 
 
Dose: 
Angina, by mouth, ADULT, initially 30 mg 3 or 4 
times daily; increase as required or at 1- 2 day 
interval until optimum response is obtained 
(maximum dose, 360 mg daily in 3- 4 divided 
doses); doses of up to 360 mg/day may be 
needed in unstable angina. 
Angina (controlled- release pro ducts), by mouth  
ADULT, initially 180 mg once daily; increase 
as required up to 360 mg once daily. 
NOTE: Dose should not be increased if heart rate drops to 
50 beats/minute. 
Hypertension, (see section below under  Anti-
Hypertensives). 
  
Dose Adjustments: 
Elderly: 
Start treatment at a lower dose. 
Renal Impairment: 
Start treatment at a lower dose. Use caution. 
Hepatic Impairment: 
For mild -to-moderate hepatic impairment, dose 
reduction may be warranted; for severe i m-
pairment, the patient should be referred to  a 
specialist. 
 
Precautions:  
 
 
WARNING: All calcium channel blockers have some degree 
of negative inotropic effect and may, in excessive 
doses or in combination with other drugs, produce 
myocardial depression especially after MI. 
 
Avoid abrupt withdrawal (has been associated with 
severe angina) and long-term use. 
May worsen first -degree AV block and exacerbate 
bradycardia; in heart failure or impaired left 
ventricular function; hepatic and renal impai r-
ment; in patients taking beta-b lockers or digi-
talis (at risk of AV block, bradycardia, asystole 
or sinus arrest) ; in patients at risk to develop 
intestinal obstruction (diltiazem has an inhibi-
tory effect on intestinal motility; may be ass o-
ciated with mood changes) ; diabetes mellitus 
(diltiazem influences insulin secretion and its 
peripheral action). 
Elderly (greater hypotensive response). 
Lactation (enters breast milk; not recommended). 
 
Adverse Drug Reactions: 
Common: Abdominal pain, bradycardia, dizziness, 
dyspnea, congestion, fatigue,  flushing, hea d-
ache, nausea, nervousness, pain, palpitations, 
peripheral edema, vasodilation, vomiting. 
Less Common: AV block, gout. 
Rare: Depression, EPS, gum hyperplasia, gynec o-
mastia, hepatitis, hypersensitivity reactions, 
photosensitivity reactions, rash. 
 
Drug Interactions:  
NOTE: Diltiazem can inhibit CYP -3A4; it may increase the 
concentration of other drugs metabolized by this e n-
zyme, possibly increasing their toxicity. 
Monitor closely with:  
Carbamazepine – its concentration and risk of 
toxicity are increased by diltiazem. 
Digoxin – its concentration and risk of toxicity may 
be increased by diltiazem. 
Drugs causing hypotension, bradycardia or slow 
cardiac conduction (e.g.,  amlodipine, diaz e-
pam, hydrochlorothiazide, methyldopa) – can 
result in increased risk of bradycardia and h y-
potension. 
Midazolam – its metabolism is inhibited by dilti-
azem, thus prolonging its sedative and respi r-
atory depressant effects. 
Phenytoin – its concentration and risk of toxicity 
may be increased by diltiazem. 
Rifampicin – its metabolism may be increased by 
diltiazem, decreasing its therapeutic effect. 
Ritonavir – its concentration and risk of adverse 
effects may be increased by diltiazem. 
Avoid concomitant use with: 
Colchicine – its concentration may be increased by 
diltiazem, with consequent toxicity. 
HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors (e.g., atorvastatin, 
simvastatin) – their concentration and the risk 
of rhabdomyolysis/myopathy may be i n-
creased by diltiazem (use the lowest effective 
dose if use cannot be avoided). 
 
Administration: Normal release preparations may 
be taken with or without food. For IR, admini s-
ter before meals and at bedtime. For long-
acting dosage forms, do not open, chew, or 
crush; swallow whole. 
NOTE: Serum levels may be elevated if taken with food.

fibrate or any component of the formulation;  
photosensitivity to ketoprofen;  p ancreatitis, 
unless due to hypertriglyceridemia;  severe r e-
nal impairment, including patients rec eiving

iary cirrhosis, gallstones or gall bladder di s-

NOTE: At least 2 -3 months of therapy is required to dete r-

By mouth (for micronized formulations), ADULT, 67 
mg up to 3 capsules/day, or 200 mg once dai-

tient response); 
By mouth  (for non -micronized formulations) ,

By mouth, CHILD, >10 years old, up to maximum of

dicated. 
Elderly: 
Use with caution; may need dose adjustment.  
 
Precautions:  
NOTE: Fenofibrate has a uricosuric effect; may reduce uric 
acid concentrations by about 25%. 
Hyperlipidemia (secondary causes of hyperlipidemia 
should be ruled out prior to therapy). 
Myopathy, myositis and rhabdomyolysis have been

comitant HMG -CoA reductase i nhibitor may 
potentiate rhabdomyolysis and lead to acute 
renal failure). 
Increase in hepatic transaminases – monitor reg u-
larly and discontinue if enzyme levels persist 3 
times above the upper limit of normal. 
Increase in serum creatinine may occur – monitor 
renal function. 
Patients at high risk for biliary tract disease, such as 
women and obese individuals (modest i n-
crease in the risk of cholesterol gallstones, r e-
flecting an increase in the cholesterol content 
of bile – may cause cholelithiasis). 
Patients at risk for venous thromboembolism ( asso-
ciated with pulmonary embolism and deep ve-
nous thrombosis). 
Avoid exposure of skin to sun, wear protective 
clothing and use sunscreen. 
Lack of optimal response (therapy should be wit h-
drawn if an adequate response is not obtained 
after 2 -3 months of therapy at the maximal 
daily dose). 
Elderly (due to a higher incident of renal impai r-
ment). 
 
Adverse Drug Reactions: 
Common: Arthralgia, bronchitis, cough, dizziness, 
fatigue, GI disturbances (e.g., dyspepsia, a b-
dominal pain); headache, hypertension, i n-
somnia, nausea, rhinitis, infections (e.g., ur i-
nary tract, respiratory tract). 
Less Common:  Pancreatitis, photosensitivity, 
pulmonary embolism, VTE. 
Rare: Agranulocytosis, anemia, arrhythmias, ch o-
lestatic jaundice, gallstones, hepatitis, hype r-
sensitivity reactions (e.g., angioedema, an a-
phylaxis, exfoliative dermatitis); hypokalemia, 
leukopenia, myopathy, rhabdomyolysis, 
thrombocytopenia. 
 
Drug Interactions: 
Monitor closely with:  
Insulins – fenofibrate may increase effects of insu-
lin. 
Statins – fenofibrate may increase the risk of my o-
pathy or rhabdomyolysis with statins. 
Sulfonylureas – fenofibrate may enhance their 
hypoglycemic effect. 
Avoid concomitant use with:  
Anticoagulants ( e.g., warfarin) – fenofibrate may 
increase their anticoagulant effect and risk of 
bleeding (monitor INR and decrease warfarin 
dose if necessary). 
Bile Acid Sequestrants – these may decrease ab-
sorption of fenofibrate. 
Administration: Best taken with food (food i n-
creases the bioavailability of fenofibrate). 
 
Pregnancy Category: C 
 
 
 
 ROSUVASTATIN 
 
Oral: 10 mg, 20 mg and 40 mg tablet  
  (as calcium salt) 
 
An active HMG -CoA reductase inhibitor , which is 
effective in reducing LDL- cholesterol concen-
tration, and has been considered to be effic a-
cious in severe hypercholesterolemia. 
 
Indications: Prevention of cardiovascular events in 
patients at high risk of a first cardiovascular 
event; in adults , for  treatment of  primary h y-

71 
 
percholesterolemia (heterozygous familial and 
non-familial hypercholesterolemia); mixed 
dyslipidemia, or homozygous familial hyper-
cholesterolemia in patients who have not r e-
sponded adequately to diet and other appr o-
priate measures; in children and adolescents  
10-17 years of age, for  treatment of heteroz y-
gous familial hypercholesterolemia. 
 
Contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to 
rosuvastatin or any component of the formul a-
tion; pregnancy (its use during first trimester 
has been associated with fetal malformation; 
decreased synthesis of c holesterol may pos-
sibly affect fetal development); breastfeeding; 
women who are planning to conceive or those 
using inadequate contraception; severe renal 
impairment; active liver disease; unexplained 
persistent elevation in serum transaminases. 
 
Dose: 
NOTE: Doses should be individualized according to the 
baseline LDL- cholesterol levels, the recommended 
goal of therapy, and patient response; adjustments 
should be made at intervals of 4 weeks or more. 
NOTE: Pharmacokinetic studies indicate that people of 
Asian ancestry may need lower doses (should start 
with 5 mg and should not receive 40 mg dose). These 
individuals may build up higher drug levels. 
High cardiovascular risk, by mouth , ADULT (men 
>50 years, women >60 years), 20 mg once 
daily.  
Hypercholesterolemia, by mouth, ADULT, initially, 5 
or 10 mg once daily (may increase the dose to 
20 mg if necessary at intervals of at least 4 
weeks); usual range, 5 -20 mg once daily 
(maximum dose, 40 mg once daily); CHILD 
10-18 years, initially 5 mg once daily (max i-
mum dose, 20 mg once daily) ; for child < 10 
years, safety and efficacy not established. 
 
Dose Adjustments: 
Renal Impairment: 
For mild-to-moderate renal impairment, dose redu c-
tion is warranted (initially 5 mg once daily; 
maximum, 10 mg once dail y); contraindicated 
in severe impairment. 
Elderly: 
Use cautiously and start at low dosage (initially 5 
mg once daily) 
Asian ancestry (may build up higher drug levels 
and be at higher risk to develop myopathy): 
Consider 5 mg as starting dose in adults. 
Patients at risk for myopathy: 
Consider lower initial dose (e.g., 5 mg/day in 
adults). 
 
Precautions:  
Renal impairment (may reduce elimination of ros u-
vastatin; impairment increases risk of myopa-
thy). 
Hepatic side effects (hepatic impairment may impair 
elimination of rosuvastatin ) – monitor liver en-
zymes before initiating therapy and if signs 
and symptoms of liver disease occur, history 
of liver disease and alcoholism.  
Myopathy may occur with lipid lowering agent; 
monitor creatine kinase ( CK) levels and di s-
continue if these are markedly elevated; with 
increased risk for rhabdomyolysis especially 
at the highest approved dose of 40 mg/day; 
reserve highest dose only for patients who fail 
to achieve desired cholesterol level at 20 
mg/day.  
Severe intercurrent illness, such as infection, trau-
ma, metabolic disorder, endocrine and elec-
trolyte disturbances, or uncontrolled seizures, 
(increases risk of myopathy; rhabdomyolysis 
and renal failure; consider withholding statin). 
Risk for d iabetes mellitus (small increases in HbA 1c 
and fasting blood glucose have been repor t-
ed); hematuria and proteinuria. 
Hypothyroidism (should be managed adequately 
before starting treatment with a statin). 
Elderly (risk of myopathy is higher especially if the 
patient is frail, age >80 years or multiple di s-
eases are present) ; children (it is important to 
establish a healthy lifestyle to reduce cardi o-
vascular risk, particularly for those with familial 
hypercholesterolemia). 
Treatment with systemic sodium fusidate (may 
increase the risk of rhabdomyolysis with a 
statin, particularly if the person is already at 
risk). 
Rare reports of immune- mediated necrotizing my o-
pathy. 
 
Adverse Drug Reactions: 
Common: Abdominal pain, arthralgia, constipation, 
diabetes mellitus, dizziness, elevated creatine 
kinase or aminotransferase concentrations, 
headache, flu-like illness,  insomnia, mild GI 
symptoms, myalgia, nausea, UTI, weakness. 
Less Common: Pruritus, rash, urticaria. 
Rare: Myopathy (including myositis), alopecia, 
amnesia, anaphylaxis, angioed ema, gynec o-
mastia, hematuria, hepatitis, hypersensitivity 
(rash, pruritus, urticaria); impotence, interstitial 
lung disease, jaundice, liver failure, pancreat i-
tis, paresthesia, peripheral neuropathy, pr o-
teinuria, rhabdomyolysis, renal failure, toxic 
epidermal necrolysis. 
 
Drug Interactions:  
NOTE: Rosuvastatin is not significantly metabolized by CYP 
enzymes. 
Monitor closely with:  
Antacids (e.g., aluminum or magnesium hydroxide) 
– these may decrease rosuvastatin concentr a-
tion (give antacid 2 hours after rosuvastatin). 
Fibrates – these increase risk of myopathy or rha b-
domyolysis with statins (the risk is less for 
fenofibrate than with gemfibrozil since it does 
not affect the concentration of statins). 
Oral Contraceptives – rosuvastatin may increase 
their serum levels. 
Avoid concomitant use with: 
Bile acid binding resins – shown to reduce GI ab -
sorption of simvastatin (give statin at least 1 
hour before, or 4 hours after, the resin). 
Niacin – increased toxicity due to pharmacodynamic 
synergism; increased risk of rhabdomyolysis 
at >1 g/day niacin. 
Warfarin – rosuvastatin may increase the INR and 
risk of bleeding (consider using other alterna-
tives).

treatment; CHF with left ventricular dysfun c-
tion following MI; diabetic nephropathy (m i-
croalbuminuria >30 mg/day) in type 1 diab e-
tes.  
 
Contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to 
captopril and other ACE inhibitors, or any 
component of the formulation; significant h y-
perkalemia; hypotension; significant bilateral 
renal artery stenosis; angioedema, including 
that related to previous treatment with an ACE 
inhibitor; pre gnancy; concurrent use with 
aliskiren in patients with DM or renal impai r-
ment.  
 
Dose:  
NOTE: Titrate dose according to patient's response; use the 
lowest effective dose. 
Heart failure, by mouth, ADULT, Initial dose: 6.25-
12.5 mg 3 times daily in conjunction with ca r-
diac glycoside and diuretic therapy (initial 
dose depends upon patient's fluid/electrolyte 
status); increased at 2-week intervals to 25-75 
mg twice daily (target dose, 50 mg 3 times 
daily). 
Hypertension, by mouth, ADULT, initial dose: 25 mg 
twice daily, may increase by 12.5- 25 mg/dose 
at 1- to 2-week intervals up to a total dose of 
150-200 mg/day in 2 divided doses. See Clini-
cal Practice Guidelines – Hypertension for fur-
ther information. 
Hypertensive urgencies, by mouth, ADULT, 25 mg 
may repeat as required. See Clinical Practice 
Guidelines – Hypertension for further info r-
mation. 
LV dysfunction following MI, by mouth, ADULT, 
Initial: 6.25 mg; if tolerated, follow with 12.5 
mg 3 times/day; then i ncrease to 25 mg 3 
times/day during next several days and then 
gradually increase over next several weeks to 
target dose of 50 mg 3 times/day (some dose 
schedules are more aggressive to achieve an 
increased goal dose within the first few days 
of initiation). 
Diabetic nephropathy, by mouth, ADULT, Initial: 25 
mg 3 times/day; may be taken with other ant i-
hypertensive therapy if required to further 
lower blood pressure. 
 
Dose Adjustments:  
Elderly:  
Start treatment with lower doses. 
Renal Impairment:  
For mild-to-moderate renal impairment, dose redu c-
tion is warranted; for severe impairment, the 
patient should be referred to a specialist. 
 
Precautions:  
 
 
WARNING: Overzealous treatment with ACE inhibitors may 
lead to sudden hypotension, renal insufficiency or 
failure, hyperkalemia with the risk of arrhythmia. 
Discontinue as soon as possible when pregnancy is detec t-
ed (may cause fetal injury or death). 
 
 
 
NOTE: ACE inhibitors should be initiated with careful clinical 
monitoring in those with severe heart failure or those: 
• Receiving multiple or high -dose diuretic therapy 
(e.g., more than 80 mg of furosemide daily or its 
equivalent); 
• With hypovolemia; 
• With hyponatremia (plasma- sodium concentration 
<130 mmol/L); 
• With hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg); 
• With unstable heart failure; 
• Receiving high-dose vasodilator therapy; 
• Known vascular disease 
Cough (dry, hacking, non- productive one , which 
usually occurs within the first few months of 
treatment and should generally resolve within 
1-4 weeks after discontinuation of the ACE in-
hibitor). 
Angioedema (may occur rarely and may involve 
head and neck , or the intestine); potential for 
myopathy increased (limit simvastatin dose to 
20 mg/day if used concurrently); peripheral 
vascular disease or generalized atheroscler o-
sis (risk of renovascular disease) ; in collagen 
vascular disease (increased risk of agranul o-
cytosis). 
Severe or symptomatic aortic stenosis (risk of hypo-
tension) and in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.  
Renal impairment (increased risk hyperkalemia and 
may affect the excr etion of ACE inhibitors) ; 
jaundice or marked elevations of hepatic en-
zymes (discontinue use due to risk of hepatic 
necrosis); surgery (excessive hypotension 
may occur during anesthesia and after sur-
gery); neutropenia and agranulocytosis; aortic 
stenosis; r enal artery stenosis; obstructive 
sleep apnea. 
Elderly (may be more predisposed to first dose 
hypotension, hyperkalemia and renovas cular 
disease than younger patients).  
Pregnancy (use in first trimester may cause major 
congenital malformations; use in sec ond and 
third trimester may cause fetal renal dysfun c-
tion and oligohydramnios, and subsequently 
fetal death) ; breastfeeding (avoid use in the 
first few weeks after delivery, particularly in 
preterm infants, due to risk of profound neona-
tal hypertension). 
NOTE: Initiation of therapy in patients with ischemic heart 
disease or cerebrovascular disease warrants close 
observation due to the potential consequences posed 
by falling blood pressure (e.g., MI, stroke). Fluid r e-
placement, if needed, may restore blood p ressure; 
therapy may then be resumed. Discontinue therapy in 
patients whose hypotension recurs. 
NOTE: Concomitant use of an angiotensin receptor blocker 
(ARB) or renin inhibitor (e.g., aliskiren) is associated 
with an increased risk of hypotension, hyperkalemia, 
and renal dysfunction. 
 
Adverse Drug Reactions:  
Common: Cough, dizziness, dysgeusia, fatigue, 
headache, hyperkalemia, hypersensitivity r e-
actions, hypertension, malaise, nausea, rash. 
Less Common:  Abnormal dreams, anaphylactoid 
reactions, angina pectoris, anorexia, chest 
pain, congestive heart failure, constipation, di-
arrhea, dryness of mouth, elevated hepatic 
aminotransferases, alkaline phosphatase and 
serum bilirubin; fever, flushing, hoarseness,  
itching, muscle cramps, MI, pallor, palpit a-
tions, Raynaud’s syndrome, sore throat, st o-
matitis, tachycardia, taste disturbances, urt i-
caria, vomiting. 
Rare: Acute renal failure, angioedema, bro n-
chospasm, cardiac arrest, cerebrovascular i n-

75 
 
sufficiency, dyspne a, erythema multiforme, 
exfoliative dermatitis, gynecomastia, hepa titis, 
hemolytic anemia, hyponatremia, impotence, 
myalgia, neuropathy, oliguria, orthostatic h y-
potension, pancreatitis, pancy topenia, poly u-
ria, proteinuria, psoriasis, renal insufficiency, 
rhythm disturbances, serum sickness -like 
syndrome, Stevens -Johnson syndrome, sy n-
cope, toxic epidermal necrolysis, visceral a n-
gioedema. 
 
Drug Interactions:  
Monitor closely with: 
Angiotensin II Receptor Blockers – these may en-
hance the adverse effects of captopril. 
Antihypertensives – captopril may enhance the 
hypotensive effects of other antihypertensives. 
Drugs which reduce blood pressure (See Appendix 
– Table D) – captopril reduces BP and, if gi v-
en with these drugs, may have additional h y-
potensive effects. 
Loop Diuretics – these increase the risk of severe 
hypotension with the first dose of an ACE i n-
hibitor, especially in patients on high- dose 
loop diuretic and/or conditions, such as heart 
failure; may increase the risk of ACE inhibitor -
induced renal impairment. 
Thiazide Diuretics – these may increase the risk of 
severe hypotension with the first dose of an 
ACE inhibitor (lesser risk than with loop diure t-
ics). 
Avoid concomitant use with: 
Antacids (e.g., aluminum or magnesium hydroxide) 
– these may decrease the serum concentr a-
tion of captopril. 
Indomethacin – this decreases the antihypertensive 
effect of captopril. 
NSAIDs (including selective COX -2 inhibitors) – 
these may reduce antihypertensive effect of 
ACE inhibitor and may increase risk of renal 
impairment and hyperkalemia. 
Potassium supplements or drugs which can cause 
potassium retention (e.g., potassium-sparing 
diuretics) – combination with these drugs may 
lead to hyperkalemia. 
Salicylates – these may reduce the antihypertensive 
effect of ACE i nhibitors; may diminish other 
beneficial pharmacodynamic effects desired 
for the treatment of congestive heart failure. 
 
Administration: Take on an empty stomach. 
NOTE: The patient may feel dizzy when taking this medicine. 
Advise the patient to get up gradually from sitting or 
lying to minimize this effect; and sit or lie down if the 
patient becomes dizzy or light-headed. 
 
Pregnancy Category: C in 1 st trimester; D in 2 nd & 
3rd trimesters. 
 
 
 
 CLONIDINE 
 
Oral: 75 microgram tablet (as hydrochloride) 
 
An imidazoline-derived, centrally -acting agonist at 
alpha2 adrenoceptors and imidazoline rece p-
tors, which acts by reducing sympathetic tone 
resulting in BP lowering. 
 
Indications: Hypertension (may be employed alone 
or used concomitantly with other antihype r-
tensive agents). 
 
Contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to 
clonidine or any component of the formulation; 
bradyarrhythmia secondary to second - or 
third-degree AV block or sick sinus syndrome. 
 
Dose: 
NOTE: The dose of clonidine should be adjusted accord ing 
to the patient’s individual BP response. 
Hypertension, by mouth , ADULT, initially 75 m i-
crograms 2-3 times daily, increased by 75 m i-
crograms daily every 2- 3 days (maximum, 1.2 
mg daily); Maintenance, 150-300 micrograms 
twice daily. 
Hypertensive urgencies, orally or sublingually, 
ADULT, 75 micrograms. See Clinical Practice 
Guidelines – Hypertension for further info r-
mation. 
 
Dose Adjustment: 
Renal Impairment: 
Dose should be adjusted according to the degree of 
impairment (patients may benefit from a lower 
initial dose); use drug with caution for mild -to-
moderate renal impairment ; for severe i m-
pairment, the patient should be referred to a 
specialist. 
 
Precautions:  
 
 
WARNING: Severe withdrawal syndrome (rebound HTN) 
may occur after abrupt discontinuation. Sudden ces-
sation of clonidine treatment has, in some cases, r e-
sulted in symptoms, such as nervousness, agitation, 
headache, and tremor accompanied or followed by a 
rapid rise in BP and elevated catecholamine concen-
tration in the plasma. 
Taper dose over 5-7 days before stopping the drug. 
 
 
 
An excessive rise in BP following discontinuation of 
clonidine therapy can be reversed by admi n-
istration of oral clonidine hydrochloride; in pa-
tients with mild -to-moderate bradyarrhythmia, 
constipation or polyneuropathy; avoid use in 
patients with depression or a history of it; 
avoid use in coronary heart disease, severe 
coronary insufficiency, conduction distur b-
ances, recent MI, cerebrovascular disease, 
Raynaud’s phenomenon or other vasospastic 
peripheral vascular disease (may be exacer-
bated by clonidine) ; diabetes  mellitus 
(clonidine may cause transient rise in blood 
glucose concentration). 
Renal impairment (clonidine may worsen chronic 
renal failure; surgery (stopping clonidine ab-
ruptly may precipitate a severe withdrawal 
syndrome; maintain treatment through periop-
erative period using parenteral dosing if nec-
essary). 
Children (commonly have GI illnesses which lead to 
vomiting; may be particularly susceptible to 
hypertensive episodes resulting from abrupt 
inability to take medication); elderly (can 
cause drowsiness). 
Pregnancy ( may lower fetal heart rate, but risk 
should be balanced against the risk of uncon-
trolled maternal hypertension; avoid IV inje c-

76 
 
tion); breastfeeding (avoid use if possible; lim-
ited data available; present in milk and may 
decrease prolactin secretion). 
Presence of a beta -blocker can worsen the wit h-
drawal syndrome (when used with beta-
blockers, withdraw clonidine over at least 7 
days after stopping the beta- blocker as r e-
bound hypertension is worse if clonidine is 
suddenly withdrawn). 
NOTE: Clonidine may make the patient feel drowsy and may 
increase the effects of alcohol. If the patient is affec t-
ed, do not drive or operate machinery.  
 
Adverse Drug Reactions: 
Common: Constipation, depression, dizziness, 
drowsiness, dry mouth, fatigue, headache, 
malaise, nausea, orthostatic hypotension, sal-
ivary gland pain, sedation, sexual dysfunction, 
sleep disturbances, vomiting. 
Less Common:  Bradycardia, confusion, delusion, 
disturbed mental state, hallucination, itching, 
nightmare, paresthesia, pruritus, rash, urticar-
ia. 
Rare: Alopecia, AV block, colonic pseudo-
obstruction, decreased lacrimation, dry eyes, 
gynecomastia, hepatitis, impaired visual a c-
commodation, nasal dryness, Raynaud’s phe-
nomenon, urinary retention. 
 
Drug Interactions:  
Monitor closely with:  
CNS depressants (e.g., alcohol, barbiturates or 
other sedating drugs) – these may enhance 
clonidine’s therapeutic and adverse effects 
(sedation, bradycardia and hypotension). 
Drugs which affect sinus node function or AV nodal 
conduction (e.g., digitalis and calcium channel 
blockers) – enhanced potential for additive ef-
fects, such as AV block and bradycardia. 
Drugs which can reduce blood pressure (See Ap-
pendix – Table D) and can slow heart rate – 
these may increase the risk and extent of h y-
potension and bradycardia. 
Avoid concomitant use with: 
Beta-blockers – combination with clonidine may 
enhance bradycardia and hypotension; may 
cause paradoxical increase in BP. 
Tricyclic A ntidepressants – these may reduce the 
hypotensive effect of clonidine, leading to i n-
creased BP. 
 
Administration: If further increments are needed 
until the desired response is achieved, taking 
the larger portion of the oral daily dose at be d-
time may minimize tra nsient adjustment ef-
fects of drowsiness and dry mouth.  
NOTE: The patient may feel dizzy when taking this medicine. 
Advise the patient to get up gradually from sitting or 
lying to minimize this effect; and sit or lie down if the 
patient becomes dizzy or light-headed. 
 
Pregnancy Category: C 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
DILTIAZEM 
 
Oral: 30 mg and 60 mg tablet (as hydrochloride) 
 60 mg, 90 mg, 120 mg and 180 mg MR cap-
sule (as hydrochloride) 
 90 mg, 120 mg and 180 mg MR tablet (as 
hydrochloride) 
 
A nondihydropyridine, calcium-channel blocker , 
which has a negative chronotropic effect, thus 
decreasing the workload of the heart , and 
consequently reducing its oxygen requir e-
ments. 
 
Indications: P rophylaxis and treatment of angina; 
hypertension. 
 
Contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to 
diltiazem or any component of the formulation; 
marked bradycardia (below 40 beats/minute ); 
sick sinus syndrome (except in the presence 
of a pacemaker); severe hypotension ( <90 
mmHg systolic); congestive heart failure; 
acute porphyria; second- or third-degree atrio-
ventricular block (without pacemaker); Wolff-
Parkinson-White syndrome; acute MI and 
pulmonary congestion; pregnancy. 
 
Dose: 
Hypertension, by mouth, ADULT, 30 mg three times 
a day (maximum dose, 360 mg daily in 3- 4 di-
vided doses). 
Hypertension (controlled- release products), by 
mouth, ADULT, initially 180-240 mg once dai-
ly; increase as required up to 360 mg once 
daily. 
 
See under the section on Anti-Anginal Agents  for 
more information. 
 
 
 
 ENALAPRIL 
 
Oral: 5 mg and 10 mg tablet (as maleate) 
 
An oral angiotensin- converting enzyme  inhibitor 
(ACEI), which is converted by hydrolysis to 
enalaprilat whose effects are similar with ca p-
topril.  
 
Indications: Hypertension; heart failure. 
 
Contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to  
enalapril and any ACE inhibitor, or any com-
ponent of the formulation; significant bilateral 
renal artery stenosis; renovascular disease; 
pregnancy; significant hyperkalemia; hypoten-
sion; history of angioedema (ACEI-induced, 
hereditary or idiopa thic); concurrent use of 
aliskiren in patients with DM or renal impai r-
ment. 
 
Dose:  
Heart failure (adjunct), asymptomatic left ventricular 
dysfunction, by mouth, ADULT, initially 2.5 mg 
once daily, increased gradually over 2 -4 
weeks to 10-20 mg twice daily if tolerated.

77 
 
Hypertension, by mouth, ADULT, initially 5 mg once 
daily (lower dose if used in addition to a di u-
retic); increased at intervals of 1- 2 weeks up 
to a total dose of 20 mg/day in 1 or 2 divided 
doses. See Clinical Practice Guidelines – H y-
pertension for further infor mation; CHILD, r e-
fer to a specialist.  
 
Dose Adjustments:  
Renal Impairment: 
For mild-to-moderate renal impairment, dose redu c-
tion is warranted; for severe impairment, the 
patient should be referred to a specialist. 
Hepatic Impairment:  
Same dosage as in patients with normal hepatic 
function may be used in patients with mild- to-
moderate hepatic impairment; for severe i m-
pairment, the patient should be referred to a 
specialist. 
 
Precautions: 
 
 
WARNING: Overzealous treatment with ACE inhibitors may 
lead to sudden hypotension, renal insufficiency or 
failure hyperkalemia with the risk of arrhythmia. 
 
 
 
 
NOTE: ACE inhibitors should be initiated with careful clinical 
monitoring in those with severe heart failure or those: 
• Receiving multiple or high-dose diuretic therapy 
(e.g., more than 80 mg of furosemide daily or its 
equivalent); 
• With hypovolemia; 
• With hyponatremia (plasma- sodium concentration 
<130 mmol/L); 
• With hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg); 
• With unstable heart failure; 
• Receiving high-dose vasodilator therapy; 
• Known vascular disease 
Cough (dry, hacking, non- productive one, which 
usually occurs within the first few months of 
treatment and should generally resolve within 
1-4 weeks after discontinuation of the ACE i n-
hibitor); concomitant use of diuretics ( see 
notes below); hypotension with the first dose, 
especially in patients: on diuretics, on a low -
sodium diet, on dialysis, if dehydrated, or with 
heart failure; peripheral vascular disease or 
generalized atherosclerosis; severe or sym p-
tomatic aortic stenosis. 
Monitor renal function before, and during, treatment; 
renal impairment (reduce dose); hepatic i m-
pairment; history of idiopathic or hereditary 
angioedema. 
Elderly (may be more predisposed to first dose 
hypotension, hyperkalemia and renovascular 
disease than younger patients). 
Pregnancy (first trimester: may cause major co n-
genital malformations; second and third tr i-
mesters: may cause fetal renal dysfunction 
and oligohydramnios, and subsequently fetal 
death); breastfeeding (amount probably too 
small to be harmful). 
USE WITH DIURETICS. Because of the risk of very rapid fall 
in blood pressure in volume- depleted patients , treat-
ment should be initiated with very low doses. High-
dose diuretic therapy (e.g., with furosemide at doses 
>80 mg daily) should be discontinued, or the dose 
significantly reduced, at least 24 hours before starting 
enalapril (may not be possible in heart failure due to 
the risk of pulmonary edema). If high- dose diuretic 
therapy cannot be stopped, medical supervis ion is 
advised for at least 2 hours after administration or un-
til blood pressure is stable. 
 
Adverse Drug Reactions:  
Common: Blurred vision, cough, depression, dizz i-
ness, dyspnea, headache, hyperkalemia, h y-
pertension. 
Less Common:  Abnormal dreams, alopecia, ana-
phylactoid reactions, angioedema, anorexia, 
chest pain, confusion, diarrhea, drowsiness, 
dry cough, dry mouth, elevated hepatic am i-
notransferases and bilirubin; fatigue, fever, 
flushing, hoarseness, hypotension, impotence, 
insomnia, muscle cramps, nausea, nervous-
ness, palpitations, rash, renal impairment, 
sweating, tinnitus, urticaria, vertigo. 
Rare: Abdominal pain, agranulocytosis, allergic 
alveolitis, aplastic anemia, arrhythmias, bron-
chospasm, electrolyte disturbances, exfoli a-
tive dermatitis, GI angioedema, gynecomastia, 
hepatitis, hemolytic anemia, hypersensitivity -
like reactions, hyponatremia, ileus, liver dam-
age, neuropathy, neutropenia, pancreatitis, 
paresthesia, peptic ulcer, proteinuria, psori a-
sis, pulmonary infiltrates, Raynaud’s sy n-
drome, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, thrombo-
cytopenia, toxic epidermal necrolysis, visceral 
angioedema, vomiting. 
 
Drug Interactions:  
Monitor closely with: 
Angiotensin II Receptor Blockers – these may en-
hance the adverse effects of enalapril.  
Antihypertensives – enalapril may enhance the 
hypotensive effects of other antihypertensives. 
Drugs which reduce blood pressure (See Appendix 
– Table D) – enalapril reduces BP and, if gi v-
en with these drugs, may have additional h y-
potensive effects. 
Loop diuretics – these increase the risk of severe 
hypotension with the first dose of an ACE i n-
hibitor, especially in patients on high- dose 
loop diuretic and/or conditions, such as heart 
failure; may increase the risk of ACE inhibitor -
induced renal impairment. 
Metformin – its hypoglycemic effect is possibly 
enhanced by enalapril. 
Thiazide Diuretics – these may increase the risk of 
severe hypotension with the first dose of an 
ACE inhibitor (less than with loop diuretics). 
Avoid concomitant use with: 
Antacids (e.g., aluminum or magnesium hydroxide) 
– may decrease the serum concentration of 
enalapril. 
Contraceptives, Oral – possible antagonism of 
hypotensive effect by estrogens.  
Indomethacin – this decreases the antihypertensive 
effects of enalapril. 
NSAIDs (including selective COX -2 inhibitors) – 
these may reduce antihypertensive effect of 
ACE inhibitor and may increase risk of renal 
impairment and hyperkalemia. 
Potassium supplements or drugs which can cause 
potassium retention (e.g., potassium-sparing 
diuretics, an d spironolactone) – combination 
with these drugs may lead to hyperkalemia. 
Salicylates – these may reduce the antihypertensive 
effect of ACE inhibitors; may diminish other

78 
 
beneficial pharmacodynamic effects desired 
for the treatment of congestive heart failure. 
 
Administration: May be taken with or without food. 
NOTE: The patient may feel dizzy when taking this medicine. 
Advise the patient to get up gradually from sitting or 
lying to minimize this effect; and sit or lie down if the 
patient becomes dizzy or light-headed. 
 
Pregnancy Category: C in 1 st trimester; D in 2 nd & 
3rd trimesters. 
 
 
 
 HYDROCHLOROTHIAZIDE 
 
Oral: 12.5 mg, 25 mg and 50 mg tablet 
 
A moderately -potent, thiazide diuretic whose max i-
mal antihypertensive effect is usually ob-
served at doses lower than those that produce 
maximal diuretic effect.  
 
Indications: Mild hypertension (alone), or in mod-
erate or severe hypertension (in combination 
with other drugs); edema. 
 
Contraindications: Severe renal impairment or 
anuria; severe hepatic impairment; hypo-
natremia, hypercalcemia, refractory hypok a-
lemia, symptomatic hyperuricemia; Addison 
disease. 
 
Dose:  
Hypertension, by mouth, ADULT, 12.5-25 mg daily, 
increased to 25- 100 mg daily if necessary; 
CHILD, refer to a specialist. See Clinical Prac-
tice Guidelines – Hypertension for further i n-
formation. 
 
Dose Adjustment:  
Renal Impairment:  
Avoid use if creatinine clearance <10 mL/minute 
 
Precautions:  
High doses of hydrochlorothiazide (may cause 
hypokalemia; if there is a history of kidney 
stones, avoid use); hypokalemia (may precipi-
tate coma – potassium-sparing diuretic can 
prevent this); gout (diuretic-induced rise in s e-
rum uric acid concentration) ; heart failure with 
significant edema (hyponatremia may occur, 
particularly if higher doses are used with a 
salt-restricted diet and/or potassium – sparing 
diuretics and excess water intake);  
Hepatic impairment (avoid in severe liver disease; in 
cirrhosis, diuretic -induced volume depletion 
and/or electrolyte disturbance – may precipi-
tate hepatic  coma or encephalopathy ); in al-
coholic cirrhosis (increased risk of hypomag-
nesemia);  
Renal impairment; electrolytes may need to be 
monitored with high doses or in renal impai r-
ment; may aggravate diabetes mellitus, gout 
and systemic lupus erythematosus; porphyria.  
Elderly (more susceptible to electrolyte imbalance 
and orthostatic hypotension). 
Pregnancy (avoid use; third trimester: may cause 
electrolyte disturbances and thrombocytop e-
nia in neonates; reduction in maternal blood 
volume may diminish uteroplacental perf u-
sion); breastfeeding (may inhibit lactation). 
 
Adverse Drug Reactions:  
Common: Dizziness, headache, hyperuricemia, 
hypochloremic alkalosis, hypokalemia, hyp o-
magnesemia, hyponatremia, lethargy, muscle 
cramps, orthostatic hypotension, polyuria, 
weakness. 
Less Common:  Blurred vision, dyslipidemia, h y-
percalcemia, hyperglycemia, impotence, rash. 
Rare: Agranulocytosis, aplastic anemia, cardiac 
arrhythmias, cholecystitis, constipation, der-
matitis, diarrhea, hemolytic anemia, hyper-
sensitivity r eactions, impotence, intrahepatic 
cholestatic jaundice, leukopenia, nausea, ne-
crotizing vasculitis, pancreatitis, purpura, 
thrombocytopenia, toxic epidermal necrolysis, 
visual disturbances, vomiting. 
 
Drug Interactions:  
Monitor closely with: 
Digoxin – hypokalemia caused by hydrochlorothi a-
zide may increase its cardiac toxicity. 
Drugs which reduce blood pressure (see Appendix 
– Table D) – thiazide diuretics reduce BP and, 
if given with these drugs, may have additional 
hypotensive effects. 
Loop Diuretics – thiazide diuretics, given with these 
drugs, have synergistic effect which may 
cause profound diuresis and serious electr o-
lyte disturbance. 
Potassium-lowering drugs – these may increase the 
possible risk of hypokalemia. 
Salbutamol – increased risk of hypokalemia with 
high doses of this drug. 
Avoid concomitant use with: 
ACE inhibitors – thiazide diuretics may increase the 
risk of severe hypotension with the first dose 
of an ACE inhibitor. 
Angiotensin II receptor blockers – treatment with 
high-dose thiazide diuretics increases risk of 
excessive hypotension after the first dose of 
an ARB (due to volume depletion). 
Contraceptives, Oral – these may antagonize the 
hypotensive effect of thiazide diuretics.  
Hydrocortisone – possible antagonism of diuretic 
effect; increased risk of hypokalemia. 
Ibuprofen – increased risk of nephrotoxicity; anta g-
onism of diuretic effect. 
Lidocaine – its effect is antagonized by hydrochlor o-
thiazide-induced hypokalemia. 
Metformin – antagonism of hypoglycemic effect. 
NSAIDs (including selective COX -2 inhibitors) – 
these may reduce: renal function, diuretic and 
hypotensive effect; and increase risk of ne-
phrotoxicity. 
 
Administration: Usually taken once daily in the 
morning. If the patient is to take it twice a day, 
take the first dose in the morning and the sec-
ond dose before 6 in the evening. 
 
Pregnancy Category: B

79

Oral: 50 mg and 100 mg tablet (as potassium salt) 
 
An angiotensin II type 1 (AT 1) receptor blocker 
whose efficacy in hypertension is similar to 
that of ACE inhibitors, but is associated with a 
lower incidence of side -effects, such as dry 
cough and angioedema. 
 
Indications: Treatment of hypertension; treatment 
of diabetic nephropathy in patients with type 2 
diabetes mellitus (noninsulin dependent,

Contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to 
losartan or any component of the formulation; 
pregnancy; concomitant use with aliskiren in

Dose:  
Hypertension, by mouth, ADULT, u sual starting 
dose: 50 mg once daily; can be administered 
once or twice daily with a total daily dose 
ranging from 25- 100 mg (usual initial dose in 
patients receiving diuretics, or those with i n-

tension for further information. 
Nephropathy in patients with type 2 DM and hyper-
tension, by mouth, ADULT, initial dose: 50 mg 
once daily; can be increased to 100 mg once

Stroke reduction (HTN with LVH), by mouth, 
ADULT, 50 mg once daily (maximum daily 
dose, 100 mg); may be used in combination 
with a thiazide diuretic. 
 
Dose Adjustments:  
Elderly:  
No initial dosage adjustment necessary. 
Renal Impairment:  
Same dosage as in patients with normal renal func-
tion may be used in patients with mild- to-
moderate renal impairment; for severe i m-
pairment, the patient should be referred to a 
specialist. 
Hepatic Impairment:  
For mild -to-moderate hepatic impairment, dose 
reduction is warranted; for severe impairment, 
the patient should be referred to a specialist. 
 
Precautions:  
 
 
WARNING: Should be discontinued when pregnancy is 
detected. 
 
 
 
Peripheral vascular disease (patients may be more 
likely to have renal artery stenosis) ; volume or 
sodium depletion (may activate the renin-
angiotensin system leading to excessive hy-
potension); caution in aortic or mitral valve 
stenosis and in hypertrophic  cardiomyopathy; 
angioedema (may occur rarely and may i n-
volve head and neck or the intestine);  hyper-
kalemia; correct volume depletion prior to ad-
ministration in patients who are salt - or vol-
ume-depleted; renal function deterioration (in-
creased risk of hype rkalemia); hepatic i m-
pairment.  
Pregnancy (avoid use unless essential; may ad-
versely affect fetal and neonatal BP con trol, 
and renal function; drugs which act on the ren-
in-angiotensin system can cause injury and

(information is limited; use better alternative

NOTE: Concomitant use of an ACE inhibitor or renin inhibitor 
(e.g., aliskiren) is associated with an increased risk of 
hypotension, hyperkalemia and renal dysfunction. 
 
Adverse Drug Reactions:  
Common: Dizziness, fatigue, headache, hyper-
kalemia, hypoglycemia, nausea, upper respi r-
atory tract infections, urinary tract infections. 
Less Common:  Abnormal liver function, angina, 
back pain, decreased hemoglobin, diarrhea, 
dyspepsia, dyspnea, edema, hypoten sion 
(first-dose), insomnia, malaise, muscle 
cramps, myalgia, nasal congestion, palpit a-
tion, pharyngitis, pruritus, rash, sleep diso r-
ders. 
Rare: Anaphylaxis, anemia, angioedema, atrial 
fibrillation, cerebrovascular accidents, cough, 
depression, erectile dysfunction, hepatitis, h y-
ponatremia, pancreatitis, purpura, renal i m-
pairment, rhabdomyolysis, syncope, thrombo-
cytopenia, vasculitis. 
 
Drug Interactions:  
Monitor closely with: 
Antifungal agents (e.g., azole derivatives, systemic) 
– these may decrease the metabolism of 
losartan, increasing its concentration and risk 
of toxicity. 
Antihypertensives – ARBs may enhance their hyp o-
tensive effect. 
Loop Diuretics – increased risk of excessive hyp o-
tension after the first dose of a n ARB because 
of volume depletion.  
NSAIDs (including selective COX -2 inhibitors) –
these may reduce the antihypertensive effect 
of losartan, and increase the risk of h yper-
kalemia or acute renal failure. 
Phenobarbital – this decreases AUC of losartan. 
Rifampicin – this may increase the metabolism of 
losartan, decreasing its concentration and af-
fecting BP control. 
Thiazide Diuretics – increased risk of excessive 
hypotension after the first dose of an ARB be-
cause of volume depletion. 
Avoid concomitant use with: 
ACE Inhibitors – angiotensin II receptor blockers 
may enhance the adverse effects of ACE i n-
hibitors.  
NOTE: Dual blockade of the Renin-Angiotensin- Aldosterone 
system (i.e., ARB and ACEI combination) in patients 
with established heart failure, atherosclerotic disease,
